mnemonics.title: Business Case Components
mnemonics.content: 'SEORCAT' - Summary, Executive overview, Options analysis, Recommendations, Costs/Benefits, Assessment (risks), Timeline

mnemonics.title: Feasibility Types
mnemonics.content: 'TELOS' - Technical, Economic, Legal, Operational, Schedule feasibility

mnemonics.title: Investment Appraisal Methods
mnemonics.content: 'NIP' - NPV, IRR, Payback period - three main financial evaluation techniques

acronyms.usage: Comparing project costs against expected benefits in monetary terms
Present value of cash inflows minus outflows; positive NPV indicates viability
Discount rate making NPV zero; compared with cost of capital for acceptance
Percentage return calculated as Net Profit divided by Investment cost
Strategic analysis framework for evaluating project and organizational factors
Project performance measurement technique combining scope, schedule, and cost

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a series of water buckets representing cash flows. Future buckets are smaller due to 'time discount' - water evaporates over time. NPV is all the water you collect after accounting for evaporation.
Think of IRR as the speed limit sign. If your project's 'speed' (IRR) exceeds the 'speed limit' (required return), you can proceed. If below, it's not worth the investment.
Use a balance scale. On one side place all costs (stones), on the other place all benefits (feathers). The project is viable if benefits weigh more.
Picture a compass where organizational strategy is North. Projects are arrows - only those pointing North (aligned) should be approved.

analogies.analogy: A business case is like a marriage proposal - it presents why two parties (organization and project) should commit, highlighting benefits, compatibility, and future plans.
NPV is like asking 'Would you rather have Rs 100 today or Rs 100 five years from now?' The discount rate answers this by showing what Rs 100 in future is worth today.
Risk assessment is like a weather forecast for your project journey - it predicts storms (threats) so you can pack accordingly or choose a different route.
Stakeholder analysis is like mapping all family members before a big decision - understanding who cares, who has influence, and who might object helps plan the approach.
